summary.overview: Indiana is in the midst of a severe energy affordability crisis, with the five investor-owned electric utilities delivering the highest year-over-year residential bill increases in at least two decades — averaging 17.5% ($28/month) in 2025 alone, according to Citizens Action Coalition analysis. NIPSCO customers saw the sharpest spike (over 90% higher than 2016 levels), prompting the IURC to launch an unprecedented Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability in March 2026, hold statewide listening sessions, and the Indiana General Assembly to pass landmark reform legislation (HEA 1002) signed by Governor Braun in February 2026. Federal threats to LIHEAP funding and ongoing major rate cases from AES Indiana and Duke Energy compound the burden on low-income Hoosier households, even as the new law introduces multi-year rate plans, summer disconnection protections for LIHEAP-enrolled customers, and mandatory low-income assistance programs.

messaging_recommendations.message: You have new rights: Indiana law now protects you from utility shutoffs during dangerous heat if you are enrolled in LIHEAP, and utilities must offer low-income assistance programs starting in 2027 — but you must enroll to be protected. Contact your utility or local Community Action Agency today to sign up for LIHEAP and levelized billing.

Indiana's energy affordability crisis is a documented, data-driven emergency — Hoosier families paid the highest year-over-year electric bill increases in 20 years in 2025, and LIHEAP federal funding is under threat. HEA 1002 is a critical first step, but legislators must close coverage gaps by expanding the summer moratorium beyond LIHEAP enrollees and removing legacy cost-tracker surcharges that inflate bills outside of rate cases.

Your soaring electric bill is not an accident: Indiana's monopoly utilities have raised rates repeatedly while passing investment risk onto you, and data centers are next in line to increase demand. The IURC's affordability inquiry and HEA 1002 are direct responses to your complaints — your voice at public hearings and comment processes has already changed outcomes.

Consumer trust in Indiana's utility system is at a historic low, as demonstrated by record complaint volumes and statewide public outcry in early 2026. Implementing HEA 1002's performance incentive mechanisms transparently, rigorously scrutinizing utility rate-of-return requests, and rapidly publishing affordability data quarterly will restore credibility and protect Hoosiers most in need.

rate_cases.description: General base rate case filed in April 2024 — Duke's first since 2019. Duke requested a ~$491.5 million revenue increase (~16.2% retail rate increase); IURC approved ~$295.7 million increase in orders from January–April 2025. Duke has filed a motion for rehearing and reconsideration.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately +$295.7 million annually (IURC-approved); Duke had requested ~$491.5 million

rate_cases.description: Duke sought rate recovery of approximately $327 million in coal ash compliance costs. The Indiana Court of Appeals ruled in August 2025 that the IURC 'impermissibly applied' Indiana law retroactively when it allowed Duke to raise rates for these retroactive coal cleanup costs, and instructed the IURC to reconsider or dismiss the application.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$62 million in collections from customers at issue; under court-ordered reconsideration

rate_cases.utility: AES Indiana (formerly Indianapolis Power & Light / IPL)
rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed a general rate case in June 2025 requesting a $192.9 million base rate increase (approximately 13.5% total in two phases). A partial settlement filed October 15, 2025, among AES Indiana, the City of Indianapolis, Walmart, and industrial customers reduced the request by 53% to $102.3 million. The OUCC opposed the settlement, recommending instead a $21.2 million rate reduction. A final IURC order was expected in spring 2026; as of mid-June 2026, a separate fuel adjustment (FAC) charge of approximately $3.98/month is in effect for June–August 2026 due to elevated winter fuel costs.
rate_cases.rate_change: Requested: +$192.9 million; Settled partial request: +$102.3 million; OUCC recommends: -$21.2 million

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — Electric
rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed for a $369 million annual electric rate increase in September 2024. A settlement agreement filed February 2025 reduced the request to approximately $257 million. The IURC approved the settlement in its entirety on June 26, 2025, resulting in a ~16.75% average residential bill increase (~$23/month for a customer using 672 kWh/month), phased in from July 2025 through early 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: +~16.75% average residential (~$23/month); approved settlement of ~$257 million annually

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — Gas
rate_cases.description: The IURC commenced an investigation in November 2025 into NIPSCO natural gas customer billing discrepancies discovered during an AMI (smart meter) rollout. NIPSCO overcharged approximately 3,500 natural gas customers due to meter index errors, provided bill credits without notifying customers or the IURC, and did not report the issue until September 2025. OUCC filed testimony in March 2026. A separate NIPSCO gas infrastructure tracker case seeks approximately $741 million in cost recovery over five years.

rate_cases.utility: CenterPoint Energy Indiana (formerly Vectren South) — Electric
rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Electric sought a $118.8 million (16.02%) base rate increase and proposed a monthly fixed charge of $23.20 (a 114% increase). The IURC approved a phased increase in a February 2025 order, with phase one effective approximately March 1, 2025, and phase two in March 2026, resulting in approximately a 20% overall bill increase for average residential customers (~$26/month by early 2026).
rate_cases.rate_change: +~20% overall for average residential customer (~$26/month by early 2026); two-phase implementation

rate_cases.description: Indiana Michigan Power announced and implemented a 3.6% decrease in base electric rates for Indiana customers beginning with the June 2026 billing cycle, following IURC approval of I&M's request to lower rates. The reduction amounts to approximately $6/month for a customer using 1,000 kWh/month. I&M also separately disclosed plans to file a new base rate case later in the summer of 2026.

articles.headline: Indiana now blocks utility shutoffs on the hottest days — but only for some customers
articles.source: WFYI / WBOI / WVXU (statewide NPR affiliate report)
articles.summary: Indiana's new law (HEA 1002) prohibits privately and cooperatively owned electric utilities from disconnecting a resident's service within 48 hours of a forecast heat index of 95°F or above — but only if the customer is enrolled in federal LIHEAP energy assistance. Critics including Indiana University professor David Konisky note the protection excludes low-income customers who qualify for assistance but have not successfully enrolled, leaving a significant gap in coverage. Indiana already prohibited winter shutoffs (Dec. 1–March 15) for LIHEAP-approved residents; the new law extends analogous protections to dangerous summer heat.

articles.headline: AES Indiana customers will see a temporary increase in bills this summer
articles.summary: AES Indiana customers face a temporary fuel adjustment charge of approximately $3.98/month (for a 1,000 kWh customer) on June–August 2026 bills due to winter fuel costs that exceeded forecasts, including costs associated with Winter Storm Fern in January 2026. The charge is separate from AES Indiana's pending base rate case (Cause No. 46258) and illustrates how tracker mechanisms can increase customer bills without a new Commission order. Citizens Action Coalition Executive Director Kerwin Olson noted the importance of tracking these additional charges beyond headline rate case figures.

articles.headline: Indiana Michigan Power to cut rates for utility customers starting in June
articles.summary: Indiana Michigan Power (I&M) announced a 3.6% decrease in electric rates for Indiana customers beginning in June 2026, following IURC approval — a rare piece of positive news for Hoosier ratepayers amid widespread rate increases. The reduction amounts to approximately $6/month for an average customer using 1,000 kWh/month. The announcement came as the IURC was continuing its statewide affordability inquiry, which received a record number of consumer complaints in early 2026.

articles.headline: IURC listening sessions make way to northeast Indiana as investigative inquiry into energy affordability continues
articles.summary: The IURC held the first of two northeast Indiana listening sessions at Syracuse Town Hall as part of its phased Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability, following the March 24 formal hearing with the 'big five' utilities. The sessions are designed to gather direct consumer input on rising bills as the Commission develops short- and long-term affordability solutions. IURC Chair Andy Zay announced plans for ten listening sessions across the state through April 2026.

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.summary: The IURC's March 24, 2026 Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability placed AES Indiana, CenterPoint Energy Indiana, Duke Energy Indiana, Indiana Michigan Power, and NIPSCO before commissioners for hours of questioning on what is driving customer bills. Utility representatives largely attributed high bills to factors including extreme weather, customer usage patterns, home insulation, and necessary infrastructure investments. IURC Chair Andy Zay signaled that inaction is not an option and announced the commission would proceed to a second phase of listening sessions statewide.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: In an unusual public move, IURC Chair Andy Zay announced the Commission would conduct an Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability, calling the 'big five' investor-owned electric utilities to appear before regulators on March 24, 2026. Zay acknowledged the action was 'not typically our style' but said Hoosiers have lost trust in their utilities and the volume of complaints from lawmakers and consumers required a direct response. The inquiry was described as the first phase of a larger strategy to address affordability drivers.

articles.headline: Your electric bill is going to change. New law aims to stabilize utilities
articles.summary: Governor Mike Braun signed House Enrolled Act 1002 into law on February 26, 2026, reshaping how Indiana's electric utilities set rates and interact with low-income customers. The law requires utilities to file three-year Multi-Year Rate Plans, establishes performance incentive mechanisms tied to affordability and reliability, mandates low-income customer assistance programs, and creates the new summer heat disconnection moratorium for LIHEAP-enrolled customers. AARP Indiana called the bill 'a step in the right direction' while noting it preferred a broader summer moratorium.

articles.headline: Hoosiers' electric bills spike 17.5% in one year
articles.summary: A Citizens Action Coalition analysis found that Indiana's five investor-owned electric utilities delivered the largest average residential bill increase in at least 20 years in 2025 — a 17.5% jump, or approximately $28 more per month. NIPSCO saw the sharpest increase (26.7%), followed by CenterPoint (24.9%), Duke (19.8%), AES Indiana (12%), and I&M (3.9%). CAC Executive Director Kerwin Olson said the increases reflect years of utility-friendly legislation that has shifted investment risk from utilities onto ratepayers.

articles.headline: NIPSCO overcharged 3,500 natural gas customers, newly unsealed records show
articles.summary: Newly unsealed IURC records revealed that NIPSCO overcharged approximately 3,500 natural gas customers due to meter index errors discovered during its AMI smart meter rollout. NIPSCO did not notify the IURC of the problem until September 2025 — months after providing silent bill credits to affected customers — and the IURC commenced a formal investigation in November 2025. For undercharged customers, NIPSCO said it would not seek repayment.

articles.headline: Indiana representative calling for review of NIPSCO rates
articles.summary: An Indiana state representative called on the IURC to review NIPSCO's rates in light of data showing NIPSCO customers using 1,000 kWh in July 2025 paid more than 90% more than in 2016, according to the IURC's own residential bill survey. The U.S. Energy Information Administration similarly identified NIPSCO as having among the highest residential electric rates in the region. The call came amid mounting complaints about NIPSCO bills from northwest Indiana residents and concurrent IURC and OUCC investigations.

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chair, Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission
stakeholders.quote: "Consider the alternative — I'll answer it kind of that way — and that's do nothing. … We will not do that." (Exact quote, in response to questions about IURC action on affordability at the March 24, 2026 Investigative Inquiry.)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle, 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chair, Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission
stakeholders.quote: "Energy affordability is one of the most pressing challenges states are facing today. We're seeing rising energy costs driven by a range of factors." (Exact quote, from Journal Gazette Five Questions interview.)
stakeholders.source: Journal Gazette, 'Five Questions for Andy Zay, Indiana Utilities Regulatory Commission chairman'

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after receiving [IURC] approval in its last rate case. My team is recommending additional reductions that would reduce the utility's current rates and help lessen the burden on ratepayers. We received thousands of consumer comments in this case, highlighting serious hardships many customers are facing." (Exact quote from OUCC news release.)

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "This is an ongoing trend of continuing to use rate payers as economic development tools while inappropriately shifting all of the investment risk of running the utility away from utilities themselves and their investors onto shareholders. And that comes at a cost, and that cost is starting to display itself as extraordinary rate increases." (Exact quote.)
stakeholders.source: Yahoo News / Hoosiers hit with historically high electric bill increases, consumer watchdog study shows

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "We appreciate the commission doing this, they are being responsive, but we are sort of asking: 'What are we doing here?' We're basically hearing the same old, same old from the utilities." (Exact quote, reacting to the March 24, 2026 IURC affordability hearing.)
stakeholders.source: AOL News / Utilities explain high energy bills to state regulators (Indianapolis Star)

stakeholders.name: Jason Tomsci
stakeholders.title: Spokesperson
stakeholders.quote: "This is a pocketbook issue for older Hoosiers, because so many of them are on that limited income. Any increase to a bill, no matter how small it might be viewed, can make a huge difference." (Exact quote, commenting on HEA 1002 signing.)
stakeholders.source: WFYI Public Media / WBOI / WVXU, 'Your electric bill is going to change. New law aims to stabilize utilities'

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor of Indiana
stakeholders.quote: "Hoosiers have been burdened with utility rate increase after increase. We can't take it anymore." (Exact quote, from September 2025 news conference announcing OUCC and IURC leadership changes.)
stakeholders.source: WFYI Public Media / Indiana Capital Chronicle, 'Braun names next top utility customer advocate, tees up regulator overhaul'

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor of Indiana
stakeholders.quote: "Affordability is the top kitchen table concern for Hoosier families. I appointed a new majority to the IURC and a new consumer advocate to take on high energy costs and ensure utilities provide service at just and reasonable rates." (Exact quote, posted on social media the day of the March 24, 2026 IURC Investigative Inquiry.)

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: "For more than a century, AES Indiana has reliably served the needs of customers across Central Indiana. Today, in 2025, the cost to deliver the essential service our customers depend on continues to rise. The rate review process ensures transparency and accountability." (Exact quote from AES Indiana press release, June 2025.)
stakeholders.source: AES Indiana press release, 'AES Indiana seeks rate review to address rising operational costs and future investments'

stakeholders.name: David Konisky
stakeholders.title: Professor, O'Neill School of Public and Environmental Affairs
stakeholders.quote: "They only are covering those who are able to successfully access the LIHEAP program, which means there are people at the same income level with the same energy assistance needs that are not going to be covered by the law." (Exact quote, commenting on the scope of Indiana's new summer shutoff protections under HEA 1002.)
stakeholders.source: WVXU / WBOI / WVPE, 'Indiana now blocks utility shutoffs on the hottest days — but only for some customers'

stakeholders.name: Bob Deig
stakeholders.title: Commissioner
stakeholders.quote: "I'd invite any of you to come to basketball games with me, or baseball games, or football games. Because everybody wants to come sit with me and talk about rates. About their energy rates." (Exact quote, spoken at the March 24–25, 2026 IURC energy affordability proceedings.)
stakeholders.source: 14 News (WFIE Evansville), 'IURC heads into second phase of energy affordability discussion'
